draft.introduction: At Theaetetus 155d, Socrates declares that wonder (thaumazein) is the beginning of philosophy. This short but foundational statement identifies the origin of philosophical inquiry in the experience of astonishment at what is.

translation.text: This is an experience which is characteristic of a philosopher, this wondering; for this is where philosophy begins, and he who made Iris the daughter of Thaumas was not a bad genealogist.
translation.original_text: μάλα γὰρ φιλοσόφου τοῦτο τὸ πάθος, τὸ θαυμάζειν· οὐ γὰρ ἄλλη ἀρχὴ φιλοσοφίας ἢ αὕτη

translator_notes.note: Aristotle echoes this claim at Metaphysics 982b12: “It is through wonder that men now begin and originally began to philosophize.” The genealogical joke refers to Hesiod’s Theogony, where Iris (messenger of the gods) is the daughter of Thaumas (“Wonder”).
